medications.indication_rationale: Simple oral analgesia is the mainstay of pain control for a superficial burn. Source note gave 1000 mg orally stat; regular dosing controls the pain that peaks in the first 24h.
medications.dose_route_frequency: 1000 mg orally stat, then 1 g four times daily as needed (maximum 4 g in 24 hours) — standard adult dosing
medications.duration: as needed for the first few days while painful

medications.indication_rationale: NSAID adjunct to paracetamol for burn pain and the associated inflammation. Source note gave 400 mg orally stat; take with food. No patient-specific contraindication reported (no renal disease, GI ulcer, asthma sensitivity, anticoagulant or pregnancy volunteered).
medications.dose_route_frequency: 400 mg orally stat with food, then 400 mg up to three times daily as needed (standard adult OTC dosing)
medications.duration: short course as needed for pain

medications.drug_name: aloe vera gel / bland fragrance-free emollient (e.g. sorbolene)
medications.indication_rationale: Liberal fragrance-free emollient or aloe vera gel soothes the intact epidermis and reduces stinging. Apply to the intact (unbroken) skin a few times daily.
medications.dose_route_frequency: apply topically to the affected intact skin three times daily

medications.drug_name: silver sulfadiazine (or other specialised burn cream)
medications.indication_rationale: Specialised antimicrobial burn creams are for deeper (partial/full-thickness) burns with a compromised barrier. The epidermis here is intact and non-blistered, so silver sulfadiazine is unnecessary and may irritate — recommending it is over-treatment.

medications.drug_name: oral antibiotics (e.g. flucloxacillin) or topical antibiotics
medications.indication_rationale: No infection is present and the skin barrier is intact — prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated for a first-degree burn and contribute to antimicrobial overuse. Antibiotics are only warranted if wound infection or cellulitis later develops.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: For the first 24 hours, lay a loose covering of plastic cling film (or a loose non-adherent dressing) over the burn to stop moving air stinging the exposed skin — apply it loosely, not tightly, and not as a first-aid wrap immediately over a fresh un-cooled burn.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Cling film is a clean, non-stick barrier that shields hypersensitive nerve endings from air currents and markedly reduces pain, without needing specialised dressings.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Wash the area normally with lukewarm water and mild soap, gently patting it dry rather than rubbing; keep it clean while it heals.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Gentle cleansing maintains hygiene without traumatising the healing epidermis; harsh rubbing delays healing.

behavioural_change_actions.specific_action: Do NOT apply ice or iced water directly to the burn at any point.
behavioural_change_actions.rationale: Direct ice causes secondary cold injury (frostbite-type damage) to already-compromised tissue and can deepen the burn.

patient_education_points.point: The boiling water has only damaged the very top protective layer of your skin. Because there are no blisters, the skin's barrier is still intact — which is why antibiotics and specialised burn creams aren't needed and could actually irritate it.
Do not put ice on the burn. Extreme cold can cause further damage to skin that's already injured. Cool running water was the right first aid.

Burns can occasionally turn out to be deeper than they first look over the next day or two. Keep an eye on it — if large blisters form, the skin turns white/leathery/black, the redness spreads up your arm, you get thick smelly yellow discharge, or you develop a fever, you need to be seen.
What to expect: the pain usually peaks in the first 24 hours then eases; the redness settles and the top layer may peel a little, with full healing in about 3-6 days and no scarring expected.

Take regular paracetamol, and ibuprofen with food, for the pain, and apply a fragrance-free moisturiser or aloe vera gel to soothe it. Covering it loosely with cling film for the first day helps if air makes it sting.

follow_up_plan.interval: Self-limiting — review with your regular GP in about 3 days ONLY if the pain is worsening, it is not settling, or delayed blistering occurs; no routine review needed if it is improving as expected
follow_up_plan.what_to_reassess: Pain trajectory, whether blistering or a deeper/wet wound bed has developed, and any signs of wound infection (spreading redness, pus, fever)
follow_up_plan.trigger_for_earlier_review: See a GP or urgent care the same day if blisters form, the skin turns white/leathery/black, redness spreads up the arm, thick foul-smelling discharge appears, or a fever develops — sooner and via 000/ED if a large area or the face/airway becomes involved

must_include_items: Reassure that this is a minor superficial (first-degree) burn manageable at home — do NOT over-triage a ~1% forearm burn to ED/urgent care
Confirm/endorse cool running water first aid (already done) and explicitly advise AGAINST applying ice
Simple analgesia — paracetamol, with ibuprofen as an adjunct (with food)
Bland fragrance-free emollient or aloe vera gel to soothe the intact skin; loose cling-film / non-adherent cover for the first 24h to reduce air-provoked pain
Safety-netting for a deeper burn or infection: blistering, skin turning white/leathery/black, spreading redness, pus, or fever
Advise that antibiotics and specialised burn creams are not needed for an intact first-degree burn
acceptable_alternatives: Paracetamol alone is acceptable if ibuprofen is contraindicated or not preferred
A simple non-adherent dressing is an acceptable alternative to cling film for covering the burn
Advising a routine GP review in ~3 days as a precaution is acceptable and not penalised as over-triage
errors_of_omission: Failing to provide any safety-netting for signs of a deeper burn or wound infection
Failing to advise against applying ice
errors_of_commission: Prescribing prophylactic oral or topical antibiotics for an intact first-degree burn
Recommending silver sulfadiazine or a specialised burn cream for an intact epidermal burn
Over-triaging a ~1% superficial forearm burn to the emergency department or calling an ambulance
Endorsing or recommending applying ice to the burn
Recommending the patient burst or deroof blisters (none are present)
scoring_rubric.passing_threshold_notes: This is a minor, self-limiting burn: the pass condition is correct self-care PLUS safety-netting for a deeper burn/infection. Assigning self-care with appropriate safety-netting is the correct answer and must PASS. Over-triage to ED/ambulance, or prescribing antibiotics/specialised burn creams, are the primary calibration failures to penalise; endorsing ice is a harmful-advice failure.
